name: Aarya Khanna
about: I work at the intersection of statistics, machine learning, and spatial data. I like problems where the model has to be clear enough to explain and honest about what it cannot claim.
My background is in high-dimensional statistics, clustering, and manifold learning; I care about methods that stay grounded in the shape of the real data.

highlights: Built classification algorithms and data pipelines to predict and optimize flavor performance using ingredient ratios and trial metadata.
Restructured trial processes to boost statistical power by blocking on key features correlating with historical flavor preferences.
Used I-optimal trial selection to identify which trials to prioritize for maximum information gain and reduced uncertainty in the model feedback loop.
experience.software: Python (Pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, matplotlib), SQL, Microsoft Office (Excel, Word, PowerPoint)
experience.methods: XGBoost/SHAP, Random Forest, logistic/linear regression, K-means clustering, ANOVA/chi-square tests, regularization, dimension reduction (UMAP, PCA)

experience.organization: UCLA Artificial Intelligence & Cultural Heritage Laboratory
highlights: Clustered questions from 15,000+ large-scale text documents into groups of semantic similarity, leveraging automated pronoun disambiguation.
Built a hybrid pipeline: initial embedding screenings to filter unlikely matches, then a cross-attention model with the right context window to maximize cluster assignment quality.

Automated cluster quality evaluation by comparing each vector’s distance to its cluster centroid, reverse-engineering a “cluster theme”, then used it as a baseline for an LLM to grade compactness with contextual reasoning.
experience.software: Python (PyTorch, Pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, matplotlib, NLTK, Hugging Face, SBERT), Linux
experience.methods: K-means / HDBSCAN / DBSCAN clustering, logistic/linear regression, LLMs, API text embeddings, dimension reduction (UMAP/PCA), silhouette scores

programming.languages: Python, SQL, R, C++, Java, Tableau, MATLAB
programming.libraries: TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, Pandas, NumPy, LangChain, Pinecone, Streamlit, SBERT/OpenAI

projects.title: Transit Deserts
bullets: LA County screening: job accessibility, desert mapping, rail corridor ranking.
Census + LEHD + GTFS; Laplacian pseudoinverse and effective resistance.
Heuristic score not a ridership or engineering model.

intro: This project is a reproducible planning screen for Los Angeles County. It estimates tract-level job accessibility by a simplified transit skim, labels high-need tracts, and ranks candidate station-to-station segments using network structure and local activity.

sections.title: Purpose
bullets: Where is cumulative job accessibility by transit (≤30 min) systematically low?
Which tracts sit in the lower tail of that distribution (“transit deserts”) for this snapshot?
Which high-activity station pairs look promising under endpoint demand × effective resistance on today’s rail graph?

sections.title: Data
bullets: Geography: LA County census tracts; consistent map bbox across figures.
Employment: LEHD WAC jobs by tract; ACS for population and median income.
Transit: GTFS feeds full stop set for accessibility skims; rail-like routes for the network graph.

sections.title: Accessibility & transit deserts
bullets: Nearest-stop skim: walk legs, in-vehicle great-circle time at an effective speed, fixed wait no mode choice or timetable routing.
Accessibility = sum of jobs reachable under a 30-minute OD time cap (cumulative opportunity).
Deserts: tracts at or below the 20th percentile of tract accessibility (relative to the modeled county).

segments.text: Notation:
is total OD time; jobs enter the sum where

paragraphs: Figure 1 pairs the accessibility surface with the binary desert layer derived from that percentile rule.

figures.alt: Tract accessibility and transit desert maps
figures.caption: Figure 1   Countywide accessibility (left) and desert mask (bottom 20%). This is the “need” view before corridor scoring.

sections.title: Rail graph & effective resistance
paragraphs: Stops from rail-family GTFS routes define nodes; consecutive stops along trips are edges (meters). Transfer links connect nearby platforms. The largest connected component is retained.

segments.text: Conductance on edge
uses distance
(m). Then
and the pseudoinverse
yield effective resistance between stations

marks pairs that are hard to connect with parallel paths on the current topology.

with tract accessibility near stations not used in the corridor rank.

segments.text: Station weight from population and jobs within
(activity proxy, not ridership).

stations, with a maximum separation cap.
Impact score:

paragraphs: Figure 4 places high-scoring segments in geographic context. It is a screening map, not a proposed alignment.

figures.caption: Figure 4: Top segments by impact score in the study area. Compare by rank rather than raw magnitude.
Top ranked corridors with endpoints, length, demand, R_eff, and impact score.

projects.title: BruinPlanner
bullets: UCLA course assistant built with retrieval-augmented generation: catalog text in, ranked courses and GE ideas out.
Streamlit UI shows match scores, readable answers, and a chat that remembers the thread for one session.
DataRes team project (Winter quarter) with Derek Shi, Yulia Anashkina, Rosie Chen, and me.

intro: BruinPlanner helps students explore UCLA courses without clicking through the whole catalog. You describe your major and what you enjoy, and the app pulls real course descriptions that are semantically close to your words, then an LLM turns those chunks into a short, structured answer.

BruinPlanner uses a straightforward RAG pipeline: embeddings plus a vector index to retrieve relevant catalog content, LangChain to organize prompts and flow, and Streamlit for a clean interactive interface.

sections.title: What it does
bullets: Input: your major (for degree context) and a free-text line about interests, such as coding, economics, or neural networks.
Output: course and GE suggestions that line up with both the wording you used and the requirements framing we encode from UCLA data.

sections.title: Retrieval augmented generation in one minute
bullets: Retrieval: turn the catalog into searchable chunks, embed your question, and fetch the pieces whose vectors sit closest to yours.
Augmentation: hand those chunks to the model as context so it cannot invent sections that do not exist in the retrieved text.
Generation: the model writes the final reply (bullets, short explanations) using only that grounded material, plus light formatting rules we add in the prompt.

sections.title: Match scores
bullets: Each course description and your interest string are embedded into the same vector space using a trained embedding model.
We rank candidates with cosine similarity. Higher similarity shows up in the UI as a higher match percentage.
Pinecone holds the vectors so lookups stay fast as the catalog grows.

sections.title: Reasoning style prompts
bullets: Chain-of-thought style prompting asks the model to spell out a few internal steps before the user-facing answer (ex: parse interests, list retrieved titles, then recommend).
The visible answer stays short; the intermediate steps mainly improve consistency and reduce shallow replies.

sections.title: Chat memory
bullets: The chatbot stores prior user and assistant messages in Streamlit session state.
Each new question can call the API with that history so follow-ups like "add a quantitative GE" build on what you already asked.

examples.title: Interest: coding and machine learning
examples.text:
User: I like coding and machine learning.

Assistant (sketch):
1) Parse interests: programming + ML.
2) Retrieve closest catalog chunks (CS intro, ML, stats).
3) Respect major filters if the user provided them.
4) Rank by embedding similarity and trim to top matches.

Top matches:
• CS M51A: Introduction to Machine Learning (match 94%)
• CS 32: Introduction to Data Structures (match 89%)
• STATS 100A: Introduction to Probability (match 84%)

Consider pairing ML with linear algebra if you have not completed that sequence yet.

examples.title: Interest: economics
examples.text:
User: I'm interested in economics!

Assistant:
• ECON 1: Principles of Microeconomics (match 91%)
• ECON 2: Principles of Macroeconomics (match 88%)
• MGMT 1A: Principles of Accounting (match 76%)

These sit on a typical economics-heavy path. Ask for GE ideas if you want breadth outside the department.

examples.title: Follow-up in the same chat
examples.text:
User: What about a GE that still feels quantitative?

Assistant:
I will search the retrieved GE-friendly entries that emphasize numbers or formal reasoning.

• STATS 12: Statistics for Life Sciences (quantitative GE)
• PHILOS 31: Logic, First Course (formal reasoning)

Choose based on whether you prefer data-style work or logic puzzles.

projects.title: Analogy LLM
bullets: Interdisciplinary teaching assistant that explains math ideas through analogies from other domains.
Generation model proposes multiple analogies; a learned reward model scores quality; PPO nudges outputs toward clearer and more faithful analogies.
RLAIF-oriented workflow designed for educational usefulness, not just fluent wording.

intro: Analogy LLM asks a simple question: can we explain hard math by connecting it to fields people already understand. The project is motivated by interdisciplinary breakthroughs, where methods from one domain unlock progress in another.

In practice, the system generates several candidate analogies, scores them for quality, and improves over time with reinforcement learning so the final answer is both understandable and faithful to the original concept.

sections.title: Project goal
bullets: Input: a math topic plus a target domain (for example chemistry, biology, or economics).
Output: an analogy that keeps the core math idea intact while translating it into familiar language.
Success criterion: the analogy should improve comprehension without introducing major conceptual drift.

sections.title: System at a high level
bullets: Generation model: Mistral-7B creates multiple candidate analogies per prompt.
Reward model: BERT-based scorer estimates which candidate is clearer, more relevant, and more accurate.
Selection loop: choose the best candidate, then use that signal to improve future generation behavior.

sections.title: RLAIF in this project
bullets: RLAIF stands for Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback: feedback comes from a model-based evaluator rather than only human labels.
Here, BERT acts as the evaluator and produces a preference signal over generated analogies.
PPO then updates the generator so higher-scoring analogy styles become more likely over training rollouts.
The practical reason for this setup is scale: it is faster to iterate than fully manual ranking while still enforcing a quality target beyond plain fluency.

sections.title: Current status
bullets: Baseline generation already produces multiple useful analogies for many prompts.
Reward-model training and PPO rollouts are the main bottleneck because evaluating several candidates per prompt is compute-heavy.
Near-term focus is stabilizing training and validating that reward gains transfer to better student-facing explanations.

examples.title: Ex: eigenvectors through protein folding intuition
examples.text:
Prompt: Explain eigenvectors using a chemistry or biology analogy.

Assistant:
Think of a protein folding process where most perturbations change the shape unpredictably, but a few privileged directions in configuration space preserve the folding pattern and only scale its intensity. Eigenvectors are those privileged directions for a linear transformation: the operation stretches or shrinks them but does not rotate them into a different direction.

examples.title: Ex: gradient descent with hiking
examples.text:
Prompt: Explain gradient descent for a beginner.

Assistant:
Imagine hiking in fog and trying to reach the valley floor. At each step you feel the local slope and move in the steepest downhill direction. Repeating that local rule gets you to a low point even without seeing the full map. Gradient descent does the same with a loss surface: it follows local slope information to reduce error step by step.

examples.title: Ex: why RLAIF changes output style
examples.text:
Prompt: Why use RLAIF for analogies?

Assistant:
A base model can produce fluent analogies, but some are shallow or slightly misleading. In RLAIF, a reward model scores candidates for clarity and faithfulness, then PPO increases the chance of high-scoring patterns. Over time, the model shifts from "sounds good" analogies to analogies that are more consistently educational.

projects.title: Vertical Los Angeles
bullets: Footprints and kriging: a height field over the city, with a simple read on how uncertain each place is.
Regression-kriging with context layers plus LEHD jobs on the same grid.
Descriptive geostatistics plus labor-market indices, not a structural urban model or causal identification stack.

intro: Vertical LA explores where Los Angeles might grow taller, using building footprints and layers like zoning, transit, and jobs. It is a descriptive, map-first story: geostatistics, a simple machine-learning trend, and a bit of urban intuition. It is not a land-use simulator or a causal claim about what drives development.
The repo is written for people who want to see how the pieces fit: data, models, and readable maps you can argue with.

sections.title: Purpose

segments.text: Estimate a smooth predicted height field
with geostatistical uncertainty, not only scattered footprints.

bullets: Quantify spatial dependence (variogram), predict on a grid (kriging), and validate with cross-validation RMSE.

segments.text: Turn predictions plus variance into interpretable indices: expected skyline zones, cluster potential in
, and (in the advanced path) tension between modeled potential and existing tall-building density.

sections.title: Data & execution
bullets: Building footprints supply height at each location; the analysis uses one consistent map projection for LA so distances and neighborhoods read the way they do on the ground.
Zoning says what is allowed where; optional rail transit and optional Census context add texture about regulation and surroundings.
Census Bureau workplace job counts (LEHD) are placed on the same grid as the height model so later maps can combine built form and employment.
Raw inputs are large and stay local; the GitHub repo ships code and a handful of example figures so the project stays easy to browse.

sections.title: How similar are nearby heights?
bullets: Nearby buildings usually have more similar heights than far-apart ones; the first chart checks that pattern and fits a smooth curve through it.
That curve is the starting point for filling in between measurements: it tells you roughly how far spatial patterns tend to reach.
When the fit is reasonable, the model can interpolate without inventing wild long-range jumps.

figures.alt: How height similarity changes with distance
figures.caption: Height difference versus distance, with fitted curves. The shape is an intuitive read on how far one building’s context typically “reaches.”

sections.title: Trend + spatial adjustment
paragraphs: First, a flexible model predicts typical height from context: zoning, distance to transit, where you are on the map, and similar inputs. That is the “what you would expect from rules and geography” layer.
A second step adds a local correction so the map respects how height actually clusters in space: expected height from context plus a neighborhood adjustment.
bullets: Cluster potential is a simple ratio: predicted height divided by predicted height plus uncertainty, so places the model is unsure about are not treated as strong hot spots.

A “new cluster” style score compares that potential to how many tall buildings already sit nearby. The idea is to highlight places where the model sees room for growth, not just where towers already stack up.

sections.title: Jobs, demand, and where work is growing
bullets: Workplace job counts from Census (LEHD) move across the map as hiring shifts; you can read where employment is clustered and how those clusters drift over time.

Jobs are a rough proxy for labor demand. Where demand is strong, there is often more pressure for office and residential space, and that pressure is part of the story people tell about cities building upward. It is not the only driver, but it is a useful lens next to zoning and the height model.
The map below shows how each cell’s job count has trended across the LEHD years (on a log scale). Warmer colors usually mean local employment has been growing; cooler colors mean decline or flatness.

figures.alt: How job counts trend over time by cell
figures.caption: Per-cell trend in log job counts over time. A simple way to see where the labor market has been heating up or cooling off on the grid.

sections.title: Spatial predictions on the grid
bullets: Each map cell carries one combined score. It folds together the skyline-style potential from the height model (prediction and uncertainty from earlier steps) with a single auxiliary outlook variable on the same grid, after normalization so the surface is easy to compare across the city.
The left panel is the raw combined score: where the model says both built-form potential and the outlook line up most strongly.

The right panel uses regularization. The strongest modeled peaks are treated as established centers, and a smooth penalty shrinks the score for cells that sit right next to those centers. The penalty eases with distance, so places farther from those peaks can still rank highly. That helps secondary or emerging areas show up instead of painting the same core over and over.

figures.caption: Unregularized combined score per cell (illustrative, not causal).
Same score after the distance-based penalty near dominant peaks: emphasis on cells that still look strong farther out.

sections.title: Reading the outputs
bullets: Exact fit metrics and rank lists depend on your footprint extract and LEHD years, so treat numbers as tied to a given run.
Compare the two prediction maps side by side: the left is the direct combined score; the right applies the regularization penalty near the strongest peaks.
Named place labels are nearest-neighbor anchors to sample points, not official boundaries. Check coordinates if you quote a location.

projects.title: Clean Your Dishes
bullets: End-to-end tool on kitchen MP4s: text-guided detection, per-object masks, and geometric labels for in-sink versus outside a user-defined basin.
Interactive OpenCV viewer with masks, boxes, sink outline, and categorized lists so results are easy to scan.
Practical runtime choices: sink-centered crop, sparse inference on frames, and configurable thresholds for real household footage.

intro: Dirty dishes getting left out is usually a roommate coordination problem, but it is hard to settle when everyone has a different memory of what happened. Clean Your Dishes turns sink-area video into a clear visual record so you can see what was left, where it was, and when it appeared.

sections.title: From sink footage to accountability
bullets: Load kitchen MP4s and define the sink once with a polygon, so every frame uses the same reference for what counts as in the basin.
Use a short text prompt (cups, bowls, plates, and similar) to find dish-like objects in each frame.
Refine each detected box into an object mask, then measure what fraction of that object lies inside the sink region.
Apply one threshold to split objects into two groups: in sink and outside.
Render the result in an OpenCV viewer with masks, boxes, the sink outline, and side lists, so anyone can quickly review likely dishes left out and connect them back to specific clips.

projects.title: TwistEd
bullets: Real-time weather alerts and safest recommended travel routes.
Interactive chatbot for historical weather questions.
Educational section on severe weather types and precautions.

intro: TwistEd combines live hazard data with routing and an educational layer so users get alerts, safer paths, and context on what the risk actually means.
The assistant path uses retrieval over historical weather records so answers stay grounded in real events rather than generic text.
detail.stack: Client requests weather alerts and route preferences; backend aggregates API feeds, scores edges on risk, and serves a small RAG-style chat index over curated historical snippets.
detail.methods: Heuristic or learned route scoring under alerts; optional NLP intent routing for the chatbot; structured cards for the education module.
frameworks: Vector store or BM25 for chat retrieval

visuals.caption: UI mock or alert + route screenshot (add file under /images/).
